structured_case.title: Ortega Dental v. Voss — defamation per se by competing practice
relief_sought: General damages presumed under defamation per se
Special damages of $140,000 for lost patient revenue
key_facts: On October 4, 2025 defendant posted on a community forum that plaintiff 'falsifies insurance billing' and 'uses unsterilized instruments.'
Plaintiff's clinic passed an unannounced Arkansas State Board of Dental Examiners inspection on October 10, 2025 with zero deficiencies.
Patient cancellations during October-November 2025 exceeded the prior-year baseline by 42% with multiple patients citing the post.
Defendant operates a competing dental practice 1.3 miles from plaintiff's office.

cases.name: Ridgeway v. State Press
cases.holding_summary: Statements imputing professional dishonesty or unfitness are defamatory per se; damages are presumed without proof of special harm.

cases.name: Atkins v. Northwest Gazette
cases.holding_summary: Fair-comment privilege protects statements of opinion based on disclosed true facts but does not shield false statements of objective fact.

cases.name: Miller v. Pioneer Investments
cases.holding_summary: Fraud in the inducement requires a material misrepresentation, scienter, reasonable reliance, and resulting damages.

cases.name: Smith v. Jones Mercantile Co.
cases.holding_summary: Where a buyer repudiates before the delivery date, the seller may treat the contract as breached and recover expectation damages measured by the contract price less resale proceeds.

favorable_precedents.name: Ridgeway v. State Press
favorable_precedents.holding_summary: Statements imputing professional dishonesty or unfitness are defamatory per se; damages are presumed without proof of special harm.

unfavorable_precedents.name: Atkins v. Northwest Gazette
unfavorable_precedents.holding_summary: Fair-comment privilege protects statements of opinion based on disclosed true facts but does not shield false statements of objective fact.

predicted_outcomes.methodology: Bayesian-style estimate from CAP precedent sample plaintiff outcomes, adjusted for party role and precedent depth.

scenarios.description: Liability finding for defamation per se with presumed and modest special damages; injunctive relief denied.
Full award including special damages of $140,000, punitive damages, and a narrow injunction.
Statements deemed opinion under fair-comment privilege; defense verdict.

predicted_outcomes.likely_judgment_summary: Liability finding for defamation per se with presumed and modest special damages; injunctive relief denied.

win_strategies.title: Frame statements as factual, not opinion
actions: Plead each statement verbatim and label its objectively verifiable nature.
Lead with Ridgeway v. State Press and distinguish Atkins on the opinion/fact line.
win_strategies.rationale: Fact characterization defeats the fair-comment privilege and supports per se treatment.

win_strategies.title: Prove falsity with regulatory record
actions: Attach the unannounced inspection report showing zero deficiencies.
Subpoena billing audits for the relevant period to rebut the 'falsifies insurance billing' claim.
win_strategies.rationale: Documentary falsity is the strongest counter to substantial-truth defenses.

win_strategies.title: Build special-damages causation
actions: Compile cancellation logs and patient declarations referencing the post.
Retain a healthcare-economics expert to model lost revenue against baseline.
win_strategies.rationale: Even with presumed damages, special damages anchor the award and settlement leverage.

win_strategies.title: Establish competitive malice for punitives
actions: Document the 1.3-mile proximity and overlapping patient market.
Develop evidence of defendant's awareness of falsity or reckless disregard.
win_strategies.rationale: Competitive motive supports the actual-malice showing punitives require.

win_strategies.title: Calibrate injunctive ask
actions: Request a narrow, content-neutral order limited to republication of identified falsehoods.
Avoid prior-restraint phrasing that triggers heightened scrutiny.
win_strategies.rationale: Overbroad injunctions risk First Amendment denial and weaken settlement posture.

favorable_judgment_tactics.detail: Move for partial summary judgment on falsity once the inspection report and billing audits are authenticated.
Anchor the case to two specific statements and three documentary records.
Lead with presumed and special damages; reserve injunctive relief as a measured ask.
Use a take-down-and-apology plus damages floor to drive early resolution.

complaint_outline.content: Ortega Dental PLLC v. Heather Voss
Plaintiff: Ortega Dental PLLC; Defendant: Dr. Heather Voss
defamation per se; tortious interference; civil conspiracy
Numbered paragraphs quoting each statement, identifying the audience, and tracing impact on patient retention.
Presumed and special damages of $140,000; punitive damages; narrow injunction

proposed_exhibits.description: Screenshots of the October 4, 2025 forum post with metadata
State Board of Dental Examiners inspection report (Oct. 10, 2025)
Patient cancellation log and supporting declarations
Key precedent: 90 Ark. 405 (1936) Ridgeway v. State Press

pre_filing_checklist: Confirm one-year defamation statute of limitations in Arkansas and any republication issues.
Verify filing fee and e-filing account for Benton County Circuit Court.
Review formatting requirements (margins, line numbering, word limits).

steps: Open e-filing portal for Circuit Court.
Upload PDF with embedded exhibits; pay filing fee.
Serve defendant per Rule 4 / local equivalent within required period.
how_to_file.note: This system does not e-file on your behalf.

top_precedents.name: Ridgeway v. State Press
top_precedents.headnotes: Statements imputing professional dishonesty or unfitness are defamatory per se; damages are presumed without proof of special harm.
top_precedents.snippet: An accusation of dishonest business practice strikes at the heart of professional standing and is actionable per se.

top_precedents.name: Atkins v. Northwest Gazette
top_precedents.headnotes: Fair-comment privilege protects statements of opinion based on disclosed true facts but does not shield false statements of objective fact.
top_precedents.snippet: Where the speaker discloses the underlying facts and labels the assessment as opinion, the privilege applies.
